      46              : /* ----------
      47              :  * Uncomment the following to enable compilation of dump_numeric()

      52              : 
      53              : 
      54              : /* ----------
      55              :  * Local data types
      56              :  *
      57              :  * Numeric values are represented in a base-NBASE floating point format.
      58              :  * Each "digit" ranges from 0 to NBASE-1.  The type NumericDigit is signed
      59              :  * and wide enough to store a digit.  We assume that NBASE*NBASE can fit in
      60              :  * an int.  Although the purely calculational routines could handle any even
      61              :  * NBASE that's less than sqrt(INT_MAX), in practice we are only interested
      62              :  * in NBASE a power of ten, so that I/O conversions and decimal rounding
      63              :  * are easy.  Also, it's actually more efficient if NBASE is rather less than
      64              :  * sqrt(INT_MAX), so that there is "headroom" for mul_var and div_var_fast to
      65              :  * postpone processing carries.
      66              :  *
      67              :  * Values of NBASE other than 10000 are considered of historical interest only
      68              :  * and are no longer supported in any sense; no mechanism exists for the client
      69              :  * to discover the base, so every client supporting binary mode expects the
      70              :  * base-10000 format.  If you plan to change this, also note the numeric
      71              :  * abbreviation code, which assumes NBASE=10000.
      72              :  * ----------
      73              :  */
      74              : 
      75              : #if 0
      76              : #define NBASE           10
      77              : #define HALF_NBASE      5
      78              : #define DEC_DIGITS      1                       /* decimal digits per NBASE digit */
      79              : #define MUL_GUARD_DIGITS        4       /* these are measured in NBASE digits */
      80              : #define DIV_GUARD_DIGITS        8
      81              : 
      82              : typedef signed char NumericDigit;
      83              : #endif
      84              : 
      85              : #if 0
      86              : #define NBASE           100
      87              : #define HALF_NBASE      50
      88              : #define DEC_DIGITS      2                       /* decimal digits per NBASE digit */
      89              : #define MUL_GUARD_DIGITS        3       /* these are measured in NBASE digits */
      90              : #define DIV_GUARD_DIGITS        6
      91              : 
      92              : typedef signed char NumericDigit;
      93              : #endif
      94              : 
      95              : #if 1
      96              : #define NBASE           10000
      97              : #define HALF_NBASE      5000
      98              : #define DEC_DIGITS      4                       /* decimal digits per NBASE digit */
      99              : #define MUL_GUARD_DIGITS        2       /* these are measured in NBASE digits */
     100              : #define DIV_GUARD_DIGITS        4
     101              : 
     102              : typedef int16 NumericDigit;
     103              : #endif
     104              : 
     105              : /*
     106              :  * The Numeric type as stored on disk.
     107              :  *
     108              :  * If the high bits of the first word of a NumericChoice (n_header, or
     109              :  * n_short.n_header, or n_long.n_sign_dscale) are NUMERIC_SHORT, then the
     110              :  * numeric follows the NumericShort format; if they are NUMERIC_POS or
     111              :  * NUMERIC_NEG, it follows the NumericLong format.  If they are NUMERIC_NAN,
     112              :  * it is a NaN.  We currently always store a NaN using just two bytes (i.e.
     113              :  * only n_header), but previous releases used only the NumericLong format,
     114              :  * so we might find 4-byte NaNs on disk if a database has been migrated using
     115              :  * pg_upgrade.  In either case, when the high bits indicate a NaN, the
     116              :  * remaining bits are never examined.  Currently, we always initialize these
     117              :  * to zero, but it might be possible to use them for some other purpose in
     118              :  * the future.
     119              :  *
     120              :  * In the NumericShort format, the remaining 14 bits of the header word
     121              :  * (n_short.n_header) are allocated as follows: 1 for sign (positive or
     122              :  * negative), 6 for dynamic scale, and 7 for weight.  In practice, most
     123              :  * commonly-encountered values can be represented this way.
     124              :  *
     125              :  * In the NumericLong format, the remaining 14 bits of the header word
     126              :  * (n_long.n_sign_dscale) represent the display scale; and the weight is
     127              :  * stored separately in n_weight.
     128              :  *
     129              :  * NOTE: by convention, values in the packed form have been stripped of
     130              :  * all leading and trailing zero digits (where a "digit" is of base NBASE).
     131              :  * In particular, if the value is zero, there will be no digits at all!
     132              :  * The weight is arbitrary in that case, but we normally set it to zero.
     133              :  */
     134              : 
     135              : struct NumericShort
     136              : {
     137              :         uint16          n_header;               /* Sign + display scale + weight */
     138              :         NumericDigit n_data[FLEXIBLE_ARRAY_MEMBER]; /* Digits */
     139              : };
     140              : 
     141              : struct NumericLong
     142              : {
     143              :         uint16          n_sign_dscale;  /* Sign + display scale */
     144              :         int16           n_weight;               /* Weight of 1st digit  */
     145              :         NumericDigit n_data[FLEXIBLE_ARRAY_MEMBER]; /* Digits */
     146              : };
     147              : 
     148              : union NumericChoice
     149              : {
     150              :         uint16          n_header;               /* Header word */
     151              :         struct NumericLong n_long;      /* Long form (4-byte header) */
     152              :         struct NumericShort n_short;    /* Short form (2-byte header) */
     153              : };
     154              : 
     155              : struct NumericData
     156              : {
     157              :         int32           vl_len_;                /* varlena header (do not touch directly!) */
     158              :         union NumericChoice choice; /* choice of format */
     159              : };
     160              : 
     161              : 
     162              : /*
     163              :  * Interpretation of high bits.
     164              :  */
     165              : 
     166              : #define NUMERIC_SIGN_MASK       0xC000
     167              : #define NUMERIC_POS                     0x0000
     168              : #define NUMERIC_NEG                     0x4000
     169              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT           0x8000
     170              : #define NUMERIC_NAN                     0xC000
     171              : 
     172              : #define NUMERIC_FLAGBITS(n) ((n)->choice.n_header & NUMERIC_SIGN_MASK)
     173              : #define NUMERIC_IS_NAN(n)               (NUMERIC_FLAGBITS(n) == NUMERIC_NAN)
     174              : #define NUMERIC_IS_SHORT(n)             (NUMERIC_FLAGBITS(n) == NUMERIC_SHORT)
     175              : 
     176              : #define NUMERIC_HDRSZ   (VARHDRSZ + sizeof(uint16) + sizeof(int16))
     177              : #define NUMERIC_HDRSZ_SHORT (VARHDRSZ + sizeof(uint16))
     178              : 
     179              : /*
     180              :  * If the flag bits are NUMERIC_SHORT or NUMERIC_NAN, we want the short header;
     181              :  * otherwise, we want the long one.  Instead of testing against each value, we
     182              :  * can just look at the high bit, for a slight efficiency gain.
     183              :  */
     184              : #define NUMERIC_HEADER_IS_SHORT(n)      (((n)->choice.n_header & 0x8000) != 0)
     185              : #define NUMERIC_HEADER_SIZE(n) \
     186              :         (VARHDRSZ + sizeof(uint16) + \
     187              :          (NUMERIC_HEADER_IS_SHORT(n) ? 0 : sizeof(int16)))
     188              : 
     189              : /*
     190              :  * Short format definitions.
     191              :  */
     192              : 
     193              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_SIGN_MASK                 0x2000
     194              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_MASK               0x1F80
     195              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_SHIFT              7
     196              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_MAX                \
     197              :         (NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_MASK >> NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_SHIFT)
     198              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_SIGN_MASK  0x0040
     199              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MASK               0x003F
     200              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MAX                NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MASK
     201              : #define NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MIN                (-(NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MASK+1))
     202              : 
     203              : /*
     204              :  * Extract sign, display scale, weight.
     205              :  */
     206              : 
     207              : #define NUMERIC_DSCALE_MASK                     0x3FFF
     208              : #define NUMERIC_DSCALE_MAX                      NUMERIC_DSCALE_MASK
     209              : 
     210              : #define NUMERIC_SIGN(n) \
     211              :         (NUMERIC_IS_SHORT(n) ? \
     212              :                 (((n)->choice.n_short.n_header & NUMERIC_SHORT_SIGN_MASK) ? \
     213              :                 NUMERIC_NEG : NUMERIC_POS) : NUMERIC_FLAGBITS(n))
     214              : #define NUMERIC_DSCALE(n)       (NUMERIC_HEADER_IS_SHORT((n)) ? \
     215              :         ((n)->choice.n_short.n_header & NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_MASK) \
     216              :                 >> NUMERIC_SHORT_DSCALE_SHIFT \
     217              :         : ((n)->choice.n_long.n_sign_dscale & NUMERIC_DSCALE_MASK))
     218              : #define NUMERIC_WEIGHT(n)       (NUMERIC_HEADER_IS_SHORT((n)) ? \
     219              :         (((n)->choice.n_short.n_header & NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_SIGN_MASK ? \
     220              :                 ~NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MASK : 0) \
     221              :          | ((n)->choice.n_short.n_header & NUMERIC_SHORT_WEIGHT_MASK)) \
     222              :         : ((n)->choice.n_long.n_weight))
     223              : 
     224              : /*
     225              :  * Maximum weight of a stored Numeric value (based on the use of int16 for the
     226              :  * weight in NumericLong).  Note that intermediate values held in NumericVar
     227              :  * and NumericSumAccum variables may have much larger weights.
     228              :  */
     229              : #define NUMERIC_WEIGHT_MAX                      PG_INT16_MAX
     230              : 
     231              : /* ----------
     232              :  * NumericVar is the format we use for arithmetic.  The digit-array part
     233              :  * is the same as the NumericData storage format, but the header is more
     234              :  * complex.
     235              :  *
     236              :  * The value represented by a NumericVar is determined by the sign, weight,
     237              :  * ndigits, and digits[] array.
     238              :  *
     239              :  * Note: the first digit of a NumericVar's value is assumed to be multiplied
     240              :  * by NBASE ** weight.  Another way to say it is that there are weight+1
     241              :  * digits before the decimal point.  It is possible to have weight < 0.
     242              :  *
     243              :  * buf points at the physical start of the palloc'd digit buffer for the
     244              :  * NumericVar.  digits points at the first digit in actual use (the one
     245              :  * with the specified weight).  We normally leave an unused digit or two
     246              :  * (preset to zeroes) between buf and digits, so that there is room to store
     247              :  * a carry out of the top digit without reallocating space.  We just need to
     248              :  * decrement digits (and increment weight) to make room for the carry digit.
     249              :  * (There is no such extra space in a numeric value stored in the database,
     250              :  * only in a NumericVar in memory.)
     251              :  *
     252              :  * If buf is NULL then the digit buffer isn't actually palloc'd and should
     253              :  * not be freed --- see the constants below for an example.
     254              :  *
     255              :  * dscale, or display scale, is the nominal precision expressed as number
     256              :  * of digits after the decimal point (it must always be >= 0 at present).
     257              :  * dscale may be more than the number of physically stored fractional digits,
     258              :  * implying that we have suppressed storage of significant trailing zeroes.
     259              :  * It should never be less than the number of stored digits, since that would
     260              :  * imply hiding digits that are present.  NOTE that dscale is always expressed
     261              :  * in *decimal* digits, and so it may correspond to a fractional number of
     262              :  * base-NBASE digits --- divide by DEC_DIGITS to convert to NBASE digits.
     263              :  *
     264              :  * rscale, or result scale, is the target precision for a computation.
     265              :  * Like dscale it is expressed as number of *decimal* digits after the decimal
     266              :  * point, and is always >= 0 at present.
     267              :  * Note that rscale is not stored in variables --- it's figured on-the-fly
     268              :  * from the dscales of the inputs.
     269              :  *
     270              :  * While we consistently use "weight" to refer to the base-NBASE weight of
     271              :  * a numeric value, it is convenient in some scale-related calculations to
     272              :  * make use of the base-10 weight (ie, the approximate log10 of the value).
     273              :  * To avoid confusion, such a decimal-units weight is called a "dweight".
     274              :  *
     275              :  * NB: All the variable-level functions are written in a style that makes it
     276              :  * possible to give one and the same variable as argument and destination.
     277              :  * This is feasible because the digit buffer is separate from the variable.
     278              :  * ----------
     279              :  */
     280              : typedef struct NumericVar
     281              : {
     282              :         int                     ndigits;                /* # of digits in digits[] - can be 0! */
     283              :         int                     weight;                 /* weight of first digit */
     284              :         int                     sign;                   /* NUMERIC_POS, NUMERIC_NEG, or NUMERIC_NAN */
     285              :         int                     dscale;                 /* display scale */
     286              :         NumericDigit *buf;                      /* start of palloc'd space for digits[] */
     287              :         NumericDigit *digits;           /* base-NBASE digits */
     288              : } NumericVar;
     289              : 
     290              : 
     291              : /* ----------
     292              :  * Data for generate_series
     293              :  * ----------
     294              :  */
     295              : typedef struct
     296              : {
     297              :         NumericVar      current;
     298              :         NumericVar      stop;
     299              :         NumericVar      step;
     300              : } generate_series_numeric_fctx;
     301              : 
     302              : 
     303              : /* ----------
     304              :  * Sort support.
     305              :  * ----------
     306              :  */
     307              : typedef struct
     308              : {
     309              :         void       *buf;                        /* buffer for short varlenas */
     310              :         int64           input_count;    /* number of non-null values seen */
     311              :         bool            estimating;             /* true if estimating cardinality */
     312              : 
     313              :         hyperLogLogState abbr_card; /* cardinality estimator */
     314              : } NumericSortSupport;
     315              : 
     316              : 
     317              : /* ----------
     318              :  * Fast sum accumulator.
     319              :  *
     320              :  * NumericSumAccum is used to implement SUM(), and other standard aggregates
     321              :  * that track the sum of input values.  It uses 32-bit integers to store the
     322              :  * digits, instead of the normal 16-bit integers (with NBASE=10000).  This
     323              :  * way, we can safely accumulate up to NBASE - 1 values without propagating
     324              :  * carry, before risking overflow of any of the digits.  'num_uncarried'
     325              :  * tracks how many values have been accumulated without propagating carry.
     326              :  *
     327              :  * Positive and negative values are accumulated separately, in 'pos_digits'
     328              :  * and 'neg_digits'.  This is simpler and faster than deciding whether to add
     329              :  * or subtract from the current value, for each new value (see sub_var() for
     330              :  * the logic we avoid by doing this).  Both buffers are of same size, and
     331              :  * have the same weight and scale.  In accum_sum_final(), the positive and
     332              :  * negative sums are added together to produce the final result.
     333              :  *
     334              :  * When a new value has a larger ndigits or weight than the accumulator
     335              :  * currently does, the accumulator is enlarged to accommodate the new value.
     336              :  * We normally have one zero digit reserved for carry propagation, and that
     337              :  * is indicated by the 'have_carry_space' flag.  When accum_sum_carry() uses
     338              :  * up the reserved digit, it clears the 'have_carry_space' flag.  The next
     339              :  * call to accum_sum_add() will enlarge the buffer, to make room for the
     340              :  * extra digit, and set the flag again.
     341              :  *
     342              :  * To initialize a new accumulator, simply reset all fields to zeros.
     343              :  *
     344              :  * The accumulator does not handle NaNs.
     345              :  * ----------
     346              :  */
     347              : typedef struct NumericSumAccum
     348              : {
     349              :         int                     ndigits;
     350              :         int                     weight;
     351              :         int                     dscale;
     352              :         int                     num_uncarried;
     353              :         bool            have_carry_space;
     354              :         int32      *pos_digits;
     355              :         int32      *neg_digits;
     356              : } NumericSumAccum;
     357              : 
     358              : 
     359              : /*
     360              :  * We define our own macros for packing and unpacking abbreviated-key
     361              :  * representations for numeric values in order to avoid depending on
     362              :  * USE_FLOAT8_BYVAL.  The type of abbreviation we use is based only on
     363              :  * the size of a datum, not the argument-passing convention for float8.
     364              :  */
     365              : #define NUMERIC_ABBREV_BITS (SIZEOF_DATUM * BITS_PER_BYTE)
     366              : #if SIZEOF_DATUM == 8
     367              : #define NumericAbbrevGetDatum(X) ((Datum) (X))
     368              : #define DatumGetNumericAbbrev(X) ((int64) (X))
     369              : #define NUMERIC_ABBREV_NAN               NumericAbbrevGetDatum(PG_INT64_MIN)
     370              : #else
     371              : #define NumericAbbrevGetDatum(X) ((Datum) (X))
     372              : #define DatumGetNumericAbbrev(X) ((int32) (X))
     373              : #define NUMERIC_ABBREV_NAN               NumericAbbrevGetDatum(PG_INT32_MIN)
     374              : #endif
     375              : 
     376              :

     391              : {1, 0, NUMERIC_POS, 0, NULL, (NumericDigit *) const_two_data};
     392              : 
     393              : #if DEC_DIGITS == 4
     394              : static const NumericDigit const_zero_point_nine_data[1] = {9000};
     395              : #elif DEC_DIGITS == 2
     396              : static const NumericDigit const_zero_point_nine_data[1] = {90};
     397              : #elif DEC_DIGITS == 1
     398              : static const NumericDigit const_zero_point_nine_data[1] = {9};
     399              : #endif
     400              : static const NumericVar const_zero_point_nine =
     401              : {1, -1, NUMERIC_POS, 1, NULL, (NumericDigit *) const_zero_point_nine_data};

     681              : /*
     682              :  * numeric_maximum_size() -
     683              :  *
     684              :  *      Maximum size of a numeric with given typmod, or -1 if unlimited/unknown.
     685              :  */
     686              : int32
     687            0 : numeric_maximum_size(int32 typmod)
     688              : {
     689              :         int                     precision;
     690              :         int                     numeric_digits;
     691              : 
     692            0 :         if (typmod < (int32) (VARHDRSZ))
     693            0 :                 return -1;
     694              : 
     695              :         /* precision (ie, max # of digits) is in upper bits of typmod */
     696            0 :         precision = ((typmod - VARHDRSZ) >> 16) & 0xffff;
     697              : 
     698              :         /*
     699              :          * This formula computes the maximum number of NumericDigits we could need
     700              :          * in order to store the specified number of decimal digits. Because the
     701              :          * weight is stored as a number of NumericDigits rather than a number of
     702              :          * decimal digits, it's possible that the first NumericDigit will contain
     703              :          * only a single decimal digit.  Thus, the first two decimal digits can
     704              :          * require two NumericDigits to store, but it isn't until we reach
     705              :          * DEC_DIGITS + 2 decimal digits that we potentially need a third
     706              :          * NumericDigit.
     707              :          */

     710              :         /*
     711              :          * In most cases, the size of a numeric will be smaller than the value
     712              :          * computed below, because the varlena header will typically get toasted

     714              :          * possible to use a short numeric header.  But our job here is to compute
     715              :          * the worst case.
     716              :          */
     717            0 :         return NUMERIC_HDRSZ + (numeric_digits * sizeof(NumericDigit));

     744              : /*
     745              :  * numeric_normalize() -

     769              :         /* If there's no decimal point, there's certainly nothing to remove. */

     771              :         {
     772              :                 /*

     774              :                  * point, this loop will terminate safely.
     775              :                  */

     779              : 
     780              :                 /* We want to get rid of the decimal point too, if it's now last. */

     782            0 :                         last--;
     783              : 
     784              :                 /* Delete whatever we backed up over. */

     791              : /*
     792              :  *              numeric_recv                    - converts external binary format to numeric

     796              :  */

     820            0 :         value.sign = (uint16) pq_getmsgint(buf, sizeof(uint16));

     822            0 :                   value.sign == NUMERIC_NEG ||
     823            0 :                   value.sign == NUMERIC_NAN))

     825              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_BINARY_REPRESENTATION),

     832              :                                  errmsg("invalid scale in external \"numeric\" value")));

     834            0 :         for (i = 0; i < len; i++)

     845              :         /*

     853            0 :         apply_typmod(&value, typmod);

     861              : /*
     862              :  *              numeric_send                    - converts numeric to binary format

     887              : /*
     888              :  * numeric_support()
     889              :  *
     890              :  * Planner support function for the numeric() length coercion function.
     891              :  *
     892              :  * Flatten calls that solely represent increases in allowable precision.
     893              :  * Scale changes mutate every datum, so they are unoptimizable.  Some values,
     894              :  * e.g. 1E-1001, can only fit into an unconstrained numeric, so a change from
     895              :  * an unconstrained numeric to any constrained numeric is also unoptimizable.
     896              :  */
     897              : Datum
     898            0 : numeric_support(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
     899              : {
     900            0 :         Node       *rawreq = (Node *) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
     901            0 :         Node       *ret = NULL;
     902              : 
     903            0 :         if (IsA(rawreq, SupportRequestSimplify))
     904              :         {
     905            0 :                 SupportRequestSimplify *req = (SupportRequestSimplify *) rawreq;
     906            0 :                 FuncExpr   *expr = req->fcall;
     907              :                 Node       *typmod;
     908              : 
     909              :                 Assert(list_length(expr->args) >= 2);
     910              : 
     911            0 :                 typmod = (Node *) lsecond(expr->args);
     912              : 
     913            0 :                 if (IsA(typmod, Const) && !((Const *) typmod)->constisnull)
     914              :                 {
     915            0 :                         Node       *source = (Node *) linitial(expr->args);
     916            0 :                         int32           old_typmod = exprTypmod(source);
     917            0 :                         int32           new_typmod = DatumGetInt32(((Const *) typmod)->constvalue);
     918            0 :                         int32           old_scale = (old_typmod - VARHDRSZ) & 0xffff;
     919            0 :                         int32           new_scale = (new_typmod - VARHDRSZ) & 0xffff;
     920            0 :                         int32           old_precision = (old_typmod - VARHDRSZ) >> 16 & 0xffff;
     921            0 :                         int32           new_precision = (new_typmod - VARHDRSZ) >> 16 & 0xffff;
     922              : 
     923              :                         /*
     924              :                          * If new_typmod < VARHDRSZ, the destination is unconstrained;
     925              :                          * that's always OK.  If old_typmod >= VARHDRSZ, the source is
     926              :                          * constrained, and we're OK if the scale is unchanged and the
     927              :                          * precision is not decreasing.  See further notes in function
     928              :                          * header comment.
     929              :                          */
     930            0 :                         if (new_typmod < (int32) VARHDRSZ ||
     931            0 :                                 (old_typmod >= (int32) VARHDRSZ &&
     932            0 :                                  new_scale == old_scale && new_precision >= old_precision))
     933            0 :                                 ret = relabel_to_typmod(source, new_typmod);
     934              :                 }
     935              :         }
     936              : 
     937            0 :         PG_RETURN_POINTER(ret);
     938              : }

     940              : /*
     941              :  * numeric() -
     942              :  *
     943              :  *      This is a special function called by the Postgres database system
     944              :  *      before a value is stored in a tuple's attribute. The precision and
     945              :  *      scale of the attribute have to be applied on the value.
     946              :  */
     947              : Datum
     948            0 : numeric         (PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
     949              : {
     950            0 :         Numeric         num = PG_GETARG_NUMERIC(0);
     951            0 :         int32           typmod = PG_GETARG_INT32(1);

     953              :         int32           tmp_typmod;
     954              :         int                     precision;
     955              :         int                     scale;
     956              :         int                     ddigits;
     957              :         int                     maxdigits;
     958              :         NumericVar      var;
     959              : 
     960              :         /*
     961              :          * Handle NaN
     962              :          */
     963            0 :         if (NUMERIC_IS_NAN(num))
     964            0 :                 PG_RETURN_NUMERIC(make_result(&const_nan));
     965              : 
     966              :         /*
     967              :          * If the value isn't a valid type modifier, simply return a copy of the
     968              :          * input value
     969              :          */

     971              :         {
     972            0 :                 new = (Numeric) palloc(VARSIZE(num));
     973            0 :                 memcpy(new, num, VARSIZE(num));
     974            0 :                 PG_RETURN_NUMERIC(new);

     976              : 
     977              :         /*
     978              :          * Get the precision and scale out of the typmod value
     979              :          */

     981            0 :         precision = (tmp_typmod >> 16) & 0xffff;
     982            0 :         scale = tmp_typmod & 0xffff;
     983            0 :         maxdigits = precision - scale;
     984              :

     987              :          * rounding could be necessary, just make a copy of the input and modify

     996              :         {

    1489              : /*
    1490              :  * Implements the numeric version of the width_bucket() function
    1491              :  * defined by SQL2003. See also width_bucket_float8().
    1492              :  *
    1493              :  * 'bound1' and 'bound2' are the lower and upper bounds of the
    1494              :  * histogram's range, respectively. 'count' is the number of buckets
    1495              :  * in the histogram. width_bucket() returns an integer indicating the
    1496              :  * bucket number that 'operand' belongs to in an equiwidth histogram
    1497              :  * with the specified characteristics. An operand smaller than the
    1498              :  * lower bound is assigned to bucket 0. An operand greater than or equal
    1499              :  * to the upper bound is assigned to an additional bucket (with number
    1500              :  * count+1). We don't allow "NaN" for any of the numeric inputs.
    1501              :  */

    1510              :         NumericVar      result_var;
    1511              :         int32           result;

    1513            0 :         if (count <= 0)

    1515              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_ARGUMENT_FOR_WIDTH_BUCKET_FUNCTION),
    1516              :                                  errmsg("count must be greater than zero")));

    1520            0 :                 NUMERIC_IS_NAN(bound2))

    1522              :                                 (errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_ARGUMENT_FOR_WIDTH_BUCKET_FUNCTION),
    1523              :                                  errmsg("operand, lower bound, and upper bound cannot be NaN")));
    1524              : 
    1525            0 :         init_var(&result_var);
    1526            0 :         init_var(&count_var);
    1527              :

    1616              : /* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    1617              :  *
    1618              :  * Comparison functions
    1619              :  *
    1620              :  * Note: btree indexes need these routines not to leak memory; therefore,
    1621              :  * be careful to free working copies of toasted datums.  Most places don't
    1622              :  * need to be so careful.
    1623              :  *
    1624              :  * Sort support:
    1625              :  *
    1626              :  * We implement the sortsupport strategy routine in order to get the benefit of
    1627              :  * abbreviation. The ordinary numeric comparison can be quite slow as a result
    1628              :  * of palloc/pfree cycles (due to detoasting packed values for alignment);
    1629              :  * while this could be worked on itself, the abbreviation strategy gives more
    1630              :  * speedup in many common cases.
    1631              :  *
    1632              :  * Two different representations are used for the abbreviated form, one in
    1633              :  * int32 and one in int64, whichever fits into a by-value Datum.  In both cases
    1634              :  * the representation is negated relative to the original value, because we use
    1635              :  * the largest negative value for NaN, which sorts higher than other values. We
    1636              :  * convert the absolute value of the numeric to a 31-bit or 63-bit positive
    1637              :  * value, and then negate it if the original number was positive.
    1638              :  *
    1639              :  * We abort the abbreviation process if the abbreviation cardinality is below
    1640              :  * 0.01% of the row count (1 per 10k non-null rows).  The actual break-even
    1641              :  * point is somewhat below that, perhaps 1 per 30k (at 1 per 100k there's a
    1642              :  * very small penalty), but we don't want to build up too many abbreviated
    1643              :  * values before first testing for abort, so we take the slightly pessimistic
    1644              :  * number.  We make no attempt to estimate the cardinality of the real values,
    1645              :  * since it plays no part in the cost model here (if the abbreviation is equal,
    1646              :  * the cost of comparing equal and unequal underlying values is comparable).
    1647              :  * We discontinue even checking for abort (saving us the hashing overhead) if
    1648              :  * the estimated cardinality gets to 100k; that would be enough to support many
    1649              :  * billions of rows while doing no worse than breaking even.
    1650              :  *
    1651              :  * ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    1652              :  */
    1653              : 
    1654              : /*
    1655              :  * Sort support strategy routine.
    1656              :  */
    1657              : Datum
    1658            0 : numeric_sortsupport(PG_FUNCTION_ARGS)
    1659              : {
    1660            0 :         SortSupport ssup = (SortSupport) PG_GETARG_POINTER(0);
    1661              : 
    1662            0 :         ssup->comparator = numeric_fast_cmp;
    1663              : 
    1664            0 :         if (ssup->abbreviate)
    1665              :         {
    1666              :                 NumericSortSupport *nss;
    1667            0 :                 MemoryContext oldcontext = MemoryContextSwitchTo(ssup->ssup_cxt);
    1668              : 
    1669            0 :                 nss = palloc(sizeof(NumericSortSupport));
    1670              : 
    1671              :                 /*
    1672              :                  * palloc a buffer for handling unaligned packed values in addition to
    1673              :                  * the support struct
    1674              :                  */
    1675            0 :                 nss->buf = palloc(VARATT_SHORT_MAX + VARHDRSZ + 1);
    1676              : 
    1677            0 :                 nss->input_count = 0;
    1678            0 :                 nss->estimating = true;
    1679            0 :                 initHyperLogLog(&nss->abbr_card, 10);
    1680              : 
    1681            0 :                 ssup->ssup_extra = nss;
    1682              : 
    1683            0 :                 ssup->abbrev_full_comparator = ssup->comparator;
    1684            0 :                 ssup->comparator = numeric_cmp_abbrev;
    1685            0 :                 ssup->abbrev_converter = numeric_abbrev_convert;
    1686            0 :                 ssup->abbrev_abort = numeric_abbrev_abort;
    1687              : 
    1688            0 :                 MemoryContextSwitchTo(oldcontext);
    1689              :         }
    1690              : 
    1691            0 :         PG_RETURN_VOID();
    1692              : }
    1693              : 
    1694              : /*
    1695              :  * Abbreviate a numeric datum, handling NaNs and detoasting
    1696              :  * (must not leak memory!)
    1697              :  */
    1698              : static Datum
    1699            0 : numeric_abbrev_convert(Datum original_datum, SortSupport ssup)

    1701            0 :         NumericSortSupport *nss = ssup->ssup_extra;

    1703              :         Numeric         value;
    1704              :         Datum           result;
    1705              : 
    1706            0 :         nss->input_count += 1;
    1707              : 
    1708              :         /*
    1709              :          * This is to handle packed datums without needing a palloc/pfree cycle;
    1710              :          * we keep and reuse a buffer large enough to handle any short datum.
    1711              :          */
    1712            0 :         if (VARATT_IS_SHORT(original_varatt))
    1713              :         {
    1714            0 :                 void       *buf = nss->buf;
    1715            0 :                 Size            sz = VARSIZE_SHORT(original_varatt) - VARHDRSZ_SHORT;
    1716              : 
    1717              :                 Assert(sz <= VARATT_SHORT_MAX - VARHDRSZ_SHORT);
    1718              : 
    1719            0 :                 SET_VARSIZE(buf, VARHDRSZ + sz);
    1720            0 :                 memcpy(VARDATA(buf), VARDATA_SHORT(original_varatt), sz);

    1723              :         }
    1724              :         else
    1725            0 :                 value = (Numeric) original_varatt;
    1726              : 
    1727            0 :         if (NUMERIC_IS_NAN(value))

    1729            0 :                 result = NUMERIC_ABBREV_NAN;
    1730              :         }

    1732              :         {
    1733              :                 NumericVar      var;
    1734              : 
    1735            0 :                 init_var_from_num(value, &var);
    1736              : 
    1737            0 :                 result = numeric_abbrev_convert_var(&var, nss);
    1738              :         }
    1739              : 
    1740              :         /* should happen only for external/compressed toasts */

    1743              : 
    1744            0 :         return result;

    1747              : /*
    1748              :  * Consider whether to abort abbreviation.
    1749              :  *
    1750              :  * We pay no attention to the cardinality of the non-abbreviated data. There is
    1751              :  * no reason to do so: unlike text, we have no fast check for equal values, so
    1752              :  * we pay the full overhead whenever the abbreviations are equal regardless of
    1753              :  * whether the underlying values are also equal.
    1754              :  */
    1755              : static bool
    1756            0 : numeric_abbrev_abort(int memtupcount, SortSupport ssup)
    1757              : {
    1758            0 :         NumericSortSupport *nss = ssup->ssup_extra;
    1759              :         double          abbr_card;
    1760              : 
    1761            0 :         if (memtupcount < 10000 || nss->input_count < 10000 || !nss->estimating)
    1762            0 :                 return false;
    1763              : 
    1764            0 :         abbr_card = estimateHyperLogLog(&nss->abbr_card);
    1765              : 
    1766              :         /*
    1767              :          * If we have >100k distinct values, then even if we were sorting many
    1768              :          * billion rows we'd likely still break even, and the penalty of undoing
    1769              :          * that many rows of abbrevs would probably not be worth it. Stop even
    1770              :          * counting at that point.
    1771              :          */
    1772            0 :         if (abbr_card > 100000.0)
    1773              :         {
    1774              : #ifdef TRACE_SORT
    1775            0 :                 if (trace_sort)
    1776            0 :                         elog(LOG,
    1777              :                                  "numeric_abbrev: estimation ends at cardinality %f"
    1778              :                                  " after " INT64_FORMAT " values (%d rows)",
    1779              :                                  abbr_card, nss->input_count, memtupcount);
    1780              : #endif
    1781            0 :                 nss->estimating = false;
    1782            0 :                 return false;
    1783              :         }
    1784              : 
    1785              :         /*
    1786              :          * Target minimum cardinality is 1 per ~10k of non-null inputs.  (The
    1787              :          * break even point is somewhere between one per 100k rows, where
    1788              :          * abbreviation has a very slight penalty, and 1 per 10k where it wins by
    1789              :          * a measurable percentage.)  We use the relatively pessimistic 10k
    1790              :          * threshold, and add a 0.5 row fudge factor, because it allows us to
    1791              :          * abort earlier on genuinely pathological data where we've had exactly
    1792              :          * one abbreviated value in the first 10k (non-null) rows.
    1793              :          */

    1795              :         {
    1796              : #ifdef TRACE_SORT
    1797            0 :                 if (trace_sort)
    1798            0 :                         elog(LOG,
    1799              :                                  "numeric_abbrev: aborting abbreviation at cardinality %f"
    1800              :                                  " below threshold %f after " INT64_FORMAT " values (%d rows)",
    1801              :                                  abbr_card, nss->input_count / 10000.0 + 0.5,
    1802              :                                  nss->input_count, memtupcount);
    1803              : #endif
    1804            0 :                 return true;
    1805              :         }
    1806              : 
    1807              : #ifdef TRACE_SORT
    1808            0 :         if (trace_sort)
    1809            0 :                 elog(LOG,
    1810              :                          "numeric_abbrev: cardinality %f"
    1811              :                          " after " INT64_FORMAT " values (%d rows)",
    1812              :                          abbr_card, nss->input_count, memtupcount);
    1813              : #endif

    1818              : /*
    1819              :  * Non-fmgr interface to the comparison routine to allow sortsupport to elide

    1863              : 
    1864              : /*
    1865              :  * Abbreviate a NumericVar according to the available bit size.
    1866              :  *
    1867              :  * The 31-bit value is constructed as:
    1868              :  *
    1869              :  *      0 + 7bits digit weight + 24 bits digit value
    1870              :  *
    1871              :  * where the digit weight is in single decimal digits, not digit words, and
    1872              :  * stored in excess-44 representation[1]. The 24-bit digit value is the 7 most
    1873              :  * significant decimal digits of the value converted to binary. Values whose
    1874              :  * weights would fall outside the representable range are rounded off to zero
    1875              :  * (which is also used to represent actual zeros) or to 0x7FFFFFFF (which
    1876              :  * otherwise cannot occur). Abbreviation therefore fails to gain any advantage
    1877              :  * where values are outside the range 10^-44 to 10^83, which is not considered
    1878              :  * to be a serious limitation, or when values are of the same magnitude and
    1879              :  * equal in the first 7 decimal digits, which is considered to be an
    1880              :  * unavoidable limitation given the available bits. (Stealing three more bits
    1881              :  * to compare another digit would narrow the range of representable weights by
    1882              :  * a factor of 8, which starts to look like a real limiting factor.)
    1883              :  *
    1884              :  * (The value 44 for the excess is essentially arbitrary)
    1885              :  *
    1886              :  * The 63-bit value is constructed as:
    1887              :  *
    1888              :  *      0 + 7bits weight + 4 x 14-bit packed digit words
    1889              :  *
    1890              :  * The weight in this case is again stored in excess-44, but this time it is
    1891              :  * the original weight in digit words (i.e. powers of 10000). The first four
    1892              :  * digit words of the value (if present; trailing zeros are assumed as needed)
    1893              :  * are packed into 14 bits each to form the rest of the value. Again,
    1894              :  * out-of-range values are rounded off to 0 or 0x7FFFFFFFFFFFFFFF. The
    1895              :  * representable range in this case is 10^-176 to 10^332, which is considered
    1896              :  * to be good enough for all practical purposes, and comparison of 4 words
    1897              :  * means that at least 13 decimal digits are compared, which is considered to
    1898              :  * be a reasonable compromise between effectiveness and efficiency in computing
    1899              :  * the abbreviation.
    1900              :  *
    1901              :  * (The value 44 for the excess is even more arbitrary here, it was chosen just
    1902              :  * to match the value used in the 31-bit case)
    1903              :  *
    1904              :  * [1] - Excess-k representation means that the value is offset by adding 'k'
    1905              :  * and then treated as unsigned, so the smallest representable value is stored
    1906              :  * with all bits zero. This allows simple comparisons to work on the composite
    1907              :  * value.
    1908              :  */
    1909              : 
    1910              : #if NUMERIC_ABBREV_BITS == 64
    1911              : 
    1912              : static Datum
    1913            0 : numeric_abbrev_convert_var(const NumericVar *var, NumericSortSupport *nss)
    1914              : {
    1915            0 :         int                     ndigits = var->ndigits;
    1916            0 :         int                     weight = var->weight;
    1917              :         int64           result;
    1918              : 
    1919            0 :         if (ndigits == 0 || weight < -44)
    1920              :         {
    1921            0 :                 result = 0;
    1922              :         }
    1923            0 :         else if (weight > 83)
    1924              :         {
    1925            0 :                 result = PG_INT64_MAX;
    1926              :         }
    1927              :         else
    1928              :         {
    1929            0 :                 result = ((int64) (weight + 44) << 56);
    1930              : 
    1931            0 :                 switch (ndigits)
    1932              :                 {
    1933            0 :                         default:
    1934            0 :                                 result |= ((int64) var->digits[3]);
    1935              :                                 /* FALLTHROUGH */
    1936            0 :                         case 3:
    1937            0 :                                 result |= ((int64) var->digits[2]) << 14;
    1938              :                                 /* FALLTHROUGH */
    1939            0 :                         case 2:
    1940            0 :                                 result |= ((int64) var->digits[1]) << 28;
    1941              :                                 /* FALLTHROUGH */

    1943            0 :                                 result |= ((int64) var->digits[0]) << 42;
    1944            0 :                                 break;
    1945              :                 }
    1946              :         }
    1947              : 
    1948              :         /* the abbrev is negated relative to the original */
    1949            0 :         if (var->sign == NUMERIC_POS)
    1950            0 :                 result = -result;
    1951              : 
    1952            0 :         if (nss->estimating)

    1954            0 :                 uint32          tmp = ((uint32) result
    1955            0 :                                                    ^ (uint32) ((uint64) result >> 32));
    1956              : 
    1957            0 :                 addHyperLogLog(&nss->abbr_card, DatumGetUInt32(hash_uint32(tmp)));
    1958              :         }
    1959              : 
    1960            0 :         return NumericAbbrevGetDatum(result);
    1961              : }

    1964              : 
    1965              : #if NUMERIC_ABBREV_BITS == 32
    1966              : 
    1967              : static Datum
    1968              : numeric_abbrev_convert_var(const NumericVar *var, NumericSortSupport *nss)
    1969              : {
    1970              :         int                     ndigits = var->ndigits;
    1971              :         int                     weight = var->weight;
    1972              :         int32           result;
    1973              : 
    1974              :         if (ndigits == 0 || weight < -11)
    1975              :         {
    1976              :                 result = 0;
    1977              :         }
    1978              :         else if (weight > 20)
    1979              :         {
    1980              :                 result = PG_INT32_MAX;
    1981              :         }
    1982              :         else

    1988              :                 result = var->digits[0];
    1989              : 
    1990              :                 /*

    1997              :                         /* already have 4 digits, add 3 more */

    2006              :                 }
    2007              :                 else if (result > 9)
    2008              :                 {

    2011              :                         /* already have 2 digits, add 5 more */

    2013              :                         weight += 1;
    2014              :                 }
    2015              :                 else
    2016              :                 {
    2017              :                         NumericDigit nxt2 = (ndigits > 2) ? var->digits[2] : 0;
    2018              : 
    2019              :                         /* already have 1 digit, add 6 more */
    2020              :                         result = (result * 1000000) + (nxt1 * 100) + (nxt2 / 100);
    2021              :                 }
    2022              : 
    2023              :                 result = result | (weight << 24);
    2024              :         }
    2025              : 
    2026              :         /* the abbrev is negated relative to the original */

    2028              :                 result = -result;
    2029              : 
    2030              :         if (nss->estimating)
    2031              :         {

    3701              : 
    3702              : /* ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    3703              :  *
    3704              :  * Aggregate functions
    3705              :  *
    3706              :  * The transition datatype for all these aggregates is declared as INTERNAL.
    3707              :  * Actually, it's a pointer to a NumericAggState allocated in the aggregate
    3708              :  * context.  The digit buffers for the NumericVars will be there too.
    3709              :  *
    3710              :  * On platforms which support 128-bit integers some aggregates instead use a
    3711              :  * 128-bit integer based transition datatype to speed up calculations.
    3712              :  *
    3713              :  * ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    3714              :  */
    3715              : 
    3716              : typedef struct NumericAggState
    3717              : {
    3718              :         bool            calcSumX2;              /* if true, calculate sumX2 */
    3719              :         MemoryContext agg_context;      /* context we're calculating in */
    3720              :         int64           N;                              /* count of processed numbers */
    3721              :         NumericSumAccum sumX;           /* sum of processed numbers */
    3722              :         NumericSumAccum sumX2;          /* sum of squares of processed numbers */
    3723              :         int                     maxScale;               /* maximum scale seen so far */
    3724              :         int64           maxScaleCount;  /* number of values seen with maximum scale */

    4364              : 
    4365              : /*
    4366              :  * Integer data types in general use Numeric accumulators to share code
    4367              :  * and avoid risk of overflow.
    4368              :  *
    4369              :  * However for performance reasons optimized special-purpose accumulator
    4370              :  * routines are used when possible.
    4371              :  *
    4372              :  * On platforms with 128-bit integer support, the 128-bit routines will be
    4373              :  * used when sum(X) or sum(X*X) fit into 128-bit.
    4374              :  *
    4375              :  * For 16 and 32 bit inputs, the N and sum(X) fit into 64-bit so the 64-bit
    4376              :  * accumulators will be used for SUM and AVG of these data types.
    4377              :  */
    4378              : 
    4379              : #ifdef HAVE_INT128
    4380              : typedef struct Int128AggState
    4381              : {
    4382              :         bool            calcSumX2;              /* if true, calculate sumX2 */
    4383              :         int64           N;                              /* count of processed numbers */
    4384              :         int128          sumX;                   /* sum of processed numbers */
    4385              :         int128          sumX2;                  /* sum of squares of processed numbers */
    4386              : } Int128AggState;
    4387              : 
    4388              : /*
    4389              :  * Prepare state data for a 128-bit aggregate function that needs to compute
    4390              :  * sum, count and optionally sum of squares of the input.
    4391              :  */

    4397              :         MemoryContext old_context;
    4398              : 
    4399            0 :         if (!AggCheckCallContext(fcinfo, &agg_context))
    4400            0 :                 elog(ERROR, "aggregate function called in non-aggregate context");
    4401              : 
    4402            0 :         old_context = MemoryContextSwitchTo(agg_context);
    4403              : 
    4404            0 :         state = (Int128AggState *) palloc0(sizeof(Int128AggState));
    4405            0 :         state->calcSumX2 = calcSumX2;
    4406              : 
    4407            0 :         MemoryContextSwitchTo(old_context);
    4408              : 
    4409            0 :         return state;
    4410              : }
    4411              : 
    4412              : /*
    4413              :  * Like makeInt128AggState(), but allocate the state in the current memory
    4414              :  * context.
    4415              :  */

    4417            0 : makeInt128AggStateCurrentContext(bool calcSumX2)
    4418              : {
    4419              :         Int128AggState *state;
    4420              : 
    4421            0 :         state = (Int128AggState *) palloc0(sizeof(Int128AggState));
    4422            0 :         state->calcSumX2 = calcSumX2;
    4423              : 
    4424            0 :         return state;
    4425              : }
    4426              : 
    4427              : /*
    4428              :  * Accumulate a new input value for 128-bit aggregate functions.
    4429              :  */
    4430              : static void
    4431            0 : do_int128_accum(Int128AggState *state, int128 newval)
    4432              : {
    4433            0 :         if (state->calcSumX2)
    4434            0 :                 state->sumX2 += newval * newval;
    4435              : 
    4436            0 :         state->sumX += newval;
    4437            0 :         state->N++;
    4438            0 : }
    4439              : 
    4440              : /*
    4441              :  * Remove an input value from the aggregated state.

    4448              :

    5165              : /*
    5166              :  * Workhorse routine for the standard deviance and variance
    5167              :  * aggregates. 'state' is aggregate's transition state.
    5168              :  * 'variance' specifies whether we should calculate the
    5169              :  * variance or the standard deviation. 'sample' indicates whether the
    5170              :  * caller is interested in the sample or the population
    5171              :  * variance/stddev.
    5172              :  *
    5173              :  * If appropriate variance statistic is undefined for the input,
    5174              :  * *is_null is set to true and NULL is returned.
    5175              :  */

    5177            0 : numeric_stddev_internal(NumericAggState *state,
    5178              :                                                 bool variance, bool sample,
    5179              :                                                 bool *is_null)
    5180              : {
    5181              :         Numeric         res;
    5182              :         NumericVar      vN,
    5183              :                                 vsumX,
    5184              :                                 vsumX2,
    5185              :                                 vNminus1;
    5186              :         const NumericVar *comp;
    5187              :         int                     rscale;
    5188              : 
    5189              :         /* Deal with empty input and NaN-input cases */
    5190            0 :         if (state == NULL || (state->N + state->NaNcount) == 0)
    5191              :         {
    5192            0 :                 *is_null = true;
    5193            0 :                 return NULL;
    5194              :         }
    5195              : 
    5196            0 :         *is_null = false;
    5197              : 
    5198            0 :         if (state->NaNcount > 0)
    5199            0 :                 return make_result(&const_nan);
    5200              : 
    5201            0 :         init_var(&vN);
    5202            0 :         init_var(&vsumX);
    5203            0 :         init_var(&vsumX2);
    5204              : 
    5205            0 :         int64_to_numericvar(state->N, &vN);
    5206            0 :         accum_sum_final(&(state->sumX), &vsumX);
    5207            0 :         accum_sum_final(&(state->sumX2), &vsumX2);
    5208              : 
    5209              :         /*
    5210              :          * Sample stddev and variance are undefined when N <= 1; population stddev
    5211              :          * is undefined when N == 0. Return NULL in either case.
    5212              :          */
    5213            0 :         if (sample)
    5214            0 :                 comp = &const_one;
    5215              :         else
    5216            0 :                 comp = &const_zero;
    5217              : 
    5218            0 :         if (cmp_var(&vN, comp) <= 0)
    5219              :         {
    5220            0 :                 *is_null = true;
    5221            0 :                 return NULL;
    5222              :         }
    5223              : 
    5224            0 :         init_var(&vNminus1);
    5225            0 :         sub_var(&vN, &const_one, &vNminus1);
    5226              : 
    5227              :         /* compute rscale for mul_var calls */
    5228            0 :         rscale = vsumX.dscale * 2;
    5229              : 
    5230            0 :         mul_var(&vsumX, &vsumX, &vsumX, rscale);    /* vsumX = sumX * sumX */
    5231            0 :         mul_var(&vN, &vsumX2, &vsumX2, rscale); /* vsumX2 = N * sumX2 */
    5232            0 :         sub_var(&vsumX2, &vsumX, &vsumX2);  /* N * sumX2 - sumX * sumX */
    5233              : 
    5234            0 :         if (cmp_var(&vsumX2, &const_zero) <= 0)
    5235              :         {
    5236              :                 /* Watch out for roundoff error producing a negative numerator */
    5237            0 :                 res = make_result(&const_zero);
    5238              :         }
    5239              :         else
    5240              :         {
    5241            0 :                 if (sample)
    5242            0 :                         mul_var(&vN, &vNminus1, &vNminus1, 0);      /* N * (N - 1) */
    5243              :                 else
    5244            0 :                         mul_var(&vN, &vN, &vNminus1, 0);    /* N * N */
    5245            0 :                 rscale = select_div_scale(&vsumX2, &vNminus1);
    5246            0 :                 div_var(&vsumX2, &vNminus1, &vsumX, rscale, true);  /* variance */
    5247            0 :                 if (!variance)
    5248            0 :                         sqrt_var(&vsumX, &vsumX, rscale);       /* stddev */
    5249              : 
    5250            0 :                 res = make_result(&vsumX);

    5458              : /*
    5459              :  * SUM transition functions for integer datatypes.
    5460              :  *
    5461              :  * To avoid overflow, we use accumulators wider than the input datatype.
    5462              :  * A Numeric accumulator is needed for int8 input; for int4 and int2
    5463              :  * inputs, we use int8 accumulators which should be sufficient for practical
    5464              :  * purposes.  (The latter two therefore don't really belong in this file,
    5465              :  * but we keep them here anyway.)
    5466              :  *
    5467              :  * Because SQL defines the SUM() of no values to be NULL, not zero,
    5468              :  * the initial condition of the transition data value needs to be NULL. This
    5469              :  * means we can't rely on ExecAgg to automatically insert the first non-null
    5470              :  * data value into the transition data: it doesn't know how to do the type
    5471              :  * conversion.  The upshot is that these routines have to be marked non-strict
    5472              :  * and handle substitution of the first non-null input themselves.
    5473              :  *
    5474              :  * Note: these functions are used only in plain aggregation mode.
    5475              :  * In moving-aggregate mode, we use intX_avg_accum and intX_avg_accum_inv.
    5476              :  */
    5477              :

    5485              :                 /* No non-null input seen so far... */
    5486            0 :                 if (PG_ARGISNULL(1))
    5487            0 :                         PG_RETURN_NULL();       /* still no non-null */
    5488              :                 /* This is the first non-null input. */
    5489            0 :                 newval = (int64) PG_GETARG_INT16(1);
    5490            0 :                 PG_RETURN_INT64(newval);
    5491              :         }
    5492              : 
    5493              :         /*
    5494              :          * If we're invoked as an aggregate, we can cheat and modify our first
    5495              :          * parameter in-place to avoid palloc overhead. If not, we need to return

    5497              :          * then of course this is useless as well as incorrect, so just ifdef it
    5498              :          * out.)
    5499              :          */

    5822              :  *
    5823              :  * Debug support

    5825              :  * ----------------------------------------------------------------------

    5827              : 
    5828              : #ifdef NUMERIC_DEBUG
    5829              : 
    5830              : /*
    5831              :  * dump_numeric() - Dump a value in the db storage format for debugging
    5832              :  */
    5833              : static void
    5834              : dump_numeric(const char *str, Numeric num)
    5835              : {
    5836              :         NumericDigit *digits = NUMERIC_DIGITS(num);
    5837              :         int                     ndigits;
    5838              :         int                     i;
    5839              : 
    5840              :         ndigits = NUMERIC_NDIGITS(num);
    5841              : 
    5842              :         printf("%s: NUMERIC w=%d d=%d ", str,
    5843              :                    NUMERIC_WEIGHT(num), NUMERIC_DSCALE(num));
    5844              :         switch (NUMERIC_SIGN(num))
    5845              :         {
    5846              :                 case NUMERIC_POS:
    5847              :                         printf("POS");
    5848              :                         break;
    5849              :                 case NUMERIC_NEG:
    5850              :                         printf("NEG");
    5851              :                         break;
    5852              :                 case NUMERIC_NAN:
    5853              :                         printf("NaN");
    5854              :                         break;
    5855              :                 default:
    5856              :                         printf("SIGN=0x%x", NUMERIC_SIGN(num));

    5866              : /*

    5883              :                 case NUMERIC_NAN:

    6338              : /*
    6339              :  * get_str_from_var_sci() -
    6340              :  *
    6341              :  *      Convert a var to a normalised scientific notation text representation.
    6342              :  *      This function does the heavy lifting for numeric_out_sci().
    6343              :  *
    6344              :  *      This notation has the general form a * 10^b, where a is known as the
    6345              :  *      "significand" and b is known as the "exponent".
    6346              :  *
    6347              :  *      Because we can't do superscript in ASCII (and because we want to copy
    6348              :  *      printf's behaviour) we display the exponent using E notation, with a
    6349              :  *      minimum of two exponent digits.
    6350              :  *
    6351              :  *      For example, the value 1234 could be output as 1.2e+03.
    6352              :  *
    6353              :  *      We assume that the exponent can fit into an int32.
    6354              :  *
    6355              :  *      rscale is the number of decimal digits desired after the decimal point in
    6356              :  *      the output, negative values will be treated as meaning zero.
    6357              :  *
    6358              :  *      Returns a palloc'd string.
    6359              :  */

    6361            0 : get_str_from_var_sci(const NumericVar *var, int rscale)

    6366              :         char       *str;
    6367              :         char       *sig_out;
    6368              :

    6370            0 :                 rscale = 0;
    6371              : 
    6372              :         /*
    6373              :          * Determine the exponent of this number in normalised form.
    6374              :          *
    6375              :          * This is the exponent required to represent the number with only one
    6376              :          * significant digit before the decimal place.
    6377              :          */
    6378            0 :         if (var->ndigits > 0)
    6379              :         {
    6380            0 :                 exponent = (var->weight + 1) * DEC_DIGITS;
    6381              : 
    6382              :                 /*
    6383              :                  * Compensate for leading decimal zeroes in the first numeric digit by
    6384              :                  * decrementing the exponent.
    6385              :                  */

    6387              :         }

    6389              :         {
    6390              :                 /*
    6391              :                  * If var has no digits, then it must be zero.
    6392              :                  *

    6398              :         }
    6399              : 
    6400              :         /*
    6401              :          * Divide var by 10^exponent to get the significand, rounding to rscale

    7240              :

    7255              :

    7281              :          * would only contribute to the right of that.  (This will give the exact
    7282              :          * rounded-to-rscale answer unless carries out of the ignored positions

    7301              : 
    7302              :         /*

    7312              :          * exceed INT_MAX - INT_MAX/NBASE.

    7334            0 :                 if (var1digit == 0)
    7335            0 :                         continue;

    7337              :                 /* Time to normalize? */

    7341              :                         /* Yes, do it */
    7342            0 :                         carry = 0;
    7343            0 :                         for (i = res_ndigits - 1; i >= 0; i--)
    7344              :                         {
    7345            0 :                                 newdig = dig[i] + carry;
    7346            0 :                                 if (newdig >= NBASE)
    7347              :                                 {
    7348            0 :                                         carry = newdig / NBASE;
    7349            0 :                                         newdig -= carry * NBASE;
    7350              :                                 }

    7352            0 :                                         carry = 0;

    7381            0 :                 newdig = dig[i] + carry;

    7383              :                 {
    7384            0 :                         carry = newdig / NBASE;
    7385            0 :                         newdig -= carry * NBASE;
    7386              :                 }

    7388            0 :                         carry = 0;

    7390              :         }
    7391              :         Assert(carry == 0);

    7408              : 
    7409              : /*
    7410              :  * div_var() -
    7411              :  *
    7412              :  *      Division on variable level. Quotient of var1 / var2 is stored in result.
    7413              :  *      The quotient is figured to exactly rscale fractional digits.
    7414              :  *      If round is true, it is rounded at the rscale'th digit; if false, it
    7415              :  *      is truncated (towards zero) at that digit.
    7416              :  */

    7434              : 
    7435              :         /* copy these values into local vars for speed in inner loop */

    7438              :

    7448              :         /*
    7449              :          * Now result zero check
    7450              :          */

    7452              :         {

    7456              :         }
    7457              : 
    7458              :         /*

    7463            1 :         if (var1->sign == var2->sign)

    7488              :          * digits) plus room for the possibly-normalized divisor (var2ndigits

    7490              :          * actual divisor data in divisor[1 .. var2ndigits].  Transferring the
    7491              :          * digits into the workspace also allows us to realloc the result (which
    7492              :          * might be the same as either input var) before we begin the main loop.
    7493              :          * Note that we use palloc0 to ensure that divisor[0], dividend[0], and
    7494              :          * any additional dividend positions beyond var1ndigits, start out 0.
    7495              :          */

    7497            1 :                 palloc0((div_ndigits + var2ndigits + 2) * sizeof(NumericDigit));
    7498            1 :         divisor = dividend + (div_ndigits + 1);
    7499            1 :         memcpy(dividend + 1, var1->digits, var1ndigits * sizeof(NumericDigit));

    7501              : 
    7502              :         /*
    7503              :          * Now we can realloc the result to hold the generated quotient digits.
    7504              :          */

    7507              : 
    7508            1 :         if (var2ndigits == 1)
    7509              :         {
    7510              :                 /*

    7512              :                  * Knuth section 4.3.1 exercise 16).
    7513              :                  */

    7515            1 :                 carry = 0;

    7517              :                 {

    7522              :         }

    7525              :                 /*

    7538            0 :                         carry = 0;

    7540              :                         {

    7551            0 :                                 dividend[i] = carry % NBASE;
    7552            0 :                                 carry = carry / NBASE;
    7553              :                         }
    7554              :                         Assert(carry == 0);
    7555              :                         Assert(divisor[1] >= HALF_NBASE);
    7556              :                 }

    7559            0 :                 divisor2 = divisor[2];
    7560              : 
    7561              :                 /*

    7563              :                  * quotient digit by dividing dividend[j .. j + var2ndigits] by the
    7564              :                  * divisor; this is essentially the same as the common manual
    7565              :                  * procedure for long division.

    7570            0 :                         int                     next2digits = dividend[j] * NBASE + dividend[j + 1];
    7571              :                         int                     qhat;

    7577              :                          * dividend.

    7581            0 :                                 res_digits[j] = 0;
    7582            0 :                                 continue;
    7583              :                         }
    7584              : 
    7585            0 :                         if (dividend[j] == divisor1)
    7586            0 :                                 qhat = NBASE - 1;
    7587              :                         else

    7589              :

    7601            0 :                         if (qhat > 0)

    7611              :                                 {
    7612            0 :                                         carry += divisor[i] * qhat;

    7617              :                                         {
    7618            0 :                                                 dividend[j + i] = borrow + NBASE;
    7619            0 :                                                 borrow = -1;
    7620              :                                         }
    7621              :                                         else
    7622              :                                         {
    7623            0 :                                                 dividend[j + i] = borrow;
    7624            0 :                                                 borrow = 0;
    7625              :                                         }
    7626              :                                 }
    7627              :                                 Assert(carry == 0);

    7632              :                                  * divisor to correct the working dividend.  (Knuth proves
    7633              :                                  * that this will occur only about 3/NBASE of the time; hence,
    7634              :                                  * it's a good idea to test this code with small NBASE to be
    7635              :                                  * sure this section gets exercised.)
    7636              :                                  */

    7638              :                                 {
    7639            0 :                                         qhat--;
    7640            0 :                                         carry = 0;
